hook_formulas.name: The Contrarian Claim
hook_formulas.template: {widely-held belief} is wrong. {the thing everyone treats as the unit} was never the unit.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Attacks a belief the audience holds without thinking about it, so they keep reading to either defend themselves or watch you back it up. Opens a clean loop with zero setup.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: the prompt was the wrong primitive. the org, not the conversation, has to remember why the work exists.

hook_formulas.name: You're Doing X Wrong
hook_formulas.template: if you {common workflow}, you already lost {the thing}. here's where it leaks.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Direct second-person accusation triggers loss aversion plus the need to check whether they're the one doing it wrong. They can't scroll until they know.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: if you hand off agents by pasting the transcript, the goal already died somewhere in the scroll.

hook_formulas.name: Naive vs Real
hook_formulas.template: what you think {X} is: {naive version}. what it actually takes: {real version}.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: The gap between the beginner mental model and the practitioner reality is inherently intriguing, and readers self-sort into 'oh, i'm the naive one' and stay to fix it.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: agent handoff, the fantasy: 'continue where you left off.' agent handoff, for real: goal + definition of done + decisions and why + budget + confidence + next action.

hook_formulas.name: The Confession
hook_formulas.template: i {embarrassing thing i believed or did} for {time}. then {the realization}.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Founder vulnerability lowers defenses and reads as honest instead of marketing. It's the building-in-public credibility engine and it earns the swipe.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: i spent months making my agents more autonomous. turns out autonomy was never the hard part. continuity was.

hook_formulas.name: The Number Nobody Expects
hook_formulas.template: {surprising true count} of {thing}. that's the actual problem.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: A concrete number is a pattern-interrupt in a feed of vague claims. Kept structural (real client count, real handoff fields) so it's credible without faking traction.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: 8 AI clients you already use. one goal. zero shared memory beneath any of them. that's the gap orgx sits in.

hook_formulas.name: Name The Enemy
hook_formulas.template: the real enemy isn't {assumed villain}. it's {the actual structural villain}.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Gives the audience a shared antagonist to rally against. The us-vs-it reframe stops the scroll because it reassigns blame away from where everyone expects.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: the enemy isn't hallucination. it's amnesia. the work forgets why it exists the second the chat closes.

hook_formulas.name: Before / After
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Transformation is visual and screenshot-worthy, and the contrast does the persuading for you. Renders cleanly as a split slide.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: before: 'here's the whole thread, good luck.' after: a compiled handoff — goal, done-criteria, permissions, budget, next action, all structured.

hook_formulas.name: Nobody Tells You
hook_formulas.template: nobody tells you that {uncomfortable truth} until {the moment the pain lands}.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Insider-secret framing implies the reader is about to get let in on something the hype cycle skips right over. Curiosity plus mild dread keeps them going.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: nobody tells you the next agent needs more than a summary. you find out when it confidently redoes work the first one already killed.

hook_formulas.name: The Teardown Promise
hook_formulas.template: i'm breaking down {specific system} in {N} slides. slide {k} is the one that'll bug you.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: An explicit promise of a structured payoff plus a forward-reference to a flagged slide forces the swipe. People chase the slide you named.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: how one person runs a fleet of agents without losing the plot. 7 slides. slide 5 is the part cursor can't do on its own.

hook_formulas.name: The Framework List
hook_formulas.template: the {N} things a {thing} needs to survive. screenshot this one.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Numbered frameworks are the most-saved carousel format, and 'screenshot this' primes the save action directly. Save-bait that also teaches.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: 7 fields every agent handoff needs to survive changing hands: goal · done-criteria · decisions and why · permissions · budget · confidence · next action.

hook_formulas.name: Hot Take, Then Proof
hook_formulas.template: {provocative one-liner}. and here's the architecture that makes it real.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: Pairs a scroll-stopping claim with a promise of receipts, pre-empting the skeptic's 'okay but prove it.' The audience is skeptical of hype but hungry for real systems, so you feed both.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: human review should scale with consequence, not activity. so tool policy gates publishing, payments + merges at the human boundary, and unknown high-risk tools fail closed.

hook_formulas.name: The Felt-Problem Cold Open
hook_formulas.template: you know that moment when {hyper-specific painful micro-scene}? that's not you. that's the primitive.
hook_formulas.why_it_works: A lived, specific scenario makes the viewer feel seen inside the first second, then absolves them and redirects the blame at the system, which is exactly the reframe the rest of the carousel pays off.
hook_formulas.example_for_orgx: that moment where you re-explain the whole goal to a fresh agent for the third time today? the chat was never built to remember it. that's the bug.

cta_bank: if your agents keep forgetting the goal, that's the whole reason orgx exists → useorgx.com
what's the worst agent handoff you've had this week? drop it, i collect these
building the shared context that lives beneath claude, cursor + the rest of them at useorgx.com
honest question: do you write a real handoff, or just paste the transcript and pray? tell me
this is one piece of it. the work graph, the quality bar, the receipts → useorgx.com
i'm building this in the open. github.com/useorgx if you want to read the actual code
reply with the one tool you'd never let an agent touch unsupervised. i want the whole list
if 'the prompt ends, the company keeps moving' lands for you, you already get orgx
more on how the quality bar loops bad work back before it wastes your attention, tomorrow. follow if agent infra is your thing
does your setup remember why the work exists, or just what you last typed into it?

format_variants.name: 6-slide hot-take → proof
format_variants.arc: slide 1 HOOK: one contrarian line that opens a loop (the prompt was the wrong primitive). slide 2: name the felt pain sharply (the work forgets why it exists when the chat closes). slide 3 TURN: the reframe (the org has to remember, not the conversation). slides 4-5 PROOF: two concrete mechanisms shown specifically — compiled handoff fields, then consequence-based escalation. slide 6: soft CTA plus a comment-bait question. fastest arc, best when you have one sharp thesis and want reach.

format_variants.name: 8-slide system teardown
format_variants.arc: slide 1 teardown-promise HOOK ('how one person runs a fleet of agents, 8 slides, slide 5 is the one'). slide 2: the felt problem. slide 3: why the obvious fix (more autonomy, better prompts) doesn't actually hold. slide 4 TURN. slides 5-7: walk the real machine one piece per slide — context pack, then work graph, then proof return — each screenshot-worthy and specific. slide 8: CTA. use when you want to show architecture depth to a skeptical builder audience.

format_variants.name: 7-slide mistake list
format_variants.arc: slide 1 HOOK: 'N ways your agent handoffs are leaking work.' slides 2-6: one mistake per slide, each paired with the fix as a tight fragment (paste-the-transcript, no done-criteria, reviewing everything equally, no tool gates, no receipt). slide 6 doubles as the save-bait recap of all the fixes. slide 7: CTA. highest save-rate format, low production effort.

format_variants.name: 9-slide founder story
format_variants.arc: slide 1 HOOK: opens on a specific scene or confession (re-explained the goal to a fresh agent three times today). slides 2-3: build the tension of the felt problem in real time. slide 4: the realization, the TURN. slides 5-7: what i built and why, concrete — name a mechanism plus a real detail (embedded MCP App widgets, fail-closed tool policy, owner-authored quality bar). slide 8: where it honestly stands, early and founder-built. slide 9: rough CTA. building-in-public register; the caption carries the full stream-of-consciousness Hope voice.

format_variants.name: 7-slide framework drop
format_variants.arc: slide 1 HOOK: 'the 7-field handoff that survives changing hands — screenshot this.' slide 2: why summaries fail (the next agent needs more than a summary). slides 3-6: build the framework field by field so each slide is a partial list the reader wants completed (goal, done-criteria, decisions and why, permissions, budget, confidence, next action). slide 6 assembles the full list = the screenshot slide. slide 7: CTA. engineered to be saved and shared, lowest effort to produce daily.

concepts.title: the prompt was the wrong primitive

slides.headline: we built everything on the wrong primitive.
slides.subtext: and we're all still building on top of it.
slides.visual_note: one huge lowercase line on near-black #02040a, a blinking terminal cursor after the period. nothing else. billboard energy.
slides.headline_original: the prompt was the wrong primitive.

slides.headline: you write a perfect prompt. the agent nails it. then the chat closes.
slides.subtext: and everything it knew closes with it.
slides.visual_note: a prompt bubble dissolving into static; a greyed-out closed browser tab in the corner.

slides.headline: the goal, the decisions, the why — gone with the transcript.
slides.subtext: the next agent starts from zero. again.
slides.visual_note: a transcript scrolling off the bottom edge into black; labeled fields (goal, why, budget) flickering out one by one.

slides.headline: the conversation was never meant to hold your company.
slides.subtext: the org has to remember why the work exists — not the chat.
slides.visual_note: the frame shifts: the chat bubble shrinks to the side, a structured instrument panel rises behind it and takes over.

slides.headline: the handoff gets compiled, not dumped.
slides.subtext: goal · definition of done · decisions+why · budget · next action
slides.visual_note: instrument-panel: five labeled fields glowing in a clean grid, structured, screenshot-worthy. this is the save slide.

slides.headline: the work lives in a graph, not a thread.
slides.subtext: initiatives → milestones → tasks → receipts. durable.
slides.visual_note: a small node graph lighting up on the left vs a dead, greyed linear chat thread on the right.

slides.headline: the prompt ends. the company keeps moving.
slides.subtext: follow if you're building agents that outlive the chat.
slides.visual_note: the quote centered, everything else dark; a small orgx wordmark bottom-center.

concepts.caption: i wrote the cleanest prompt of my life last tuesday. genuinely proud of it. the agent shipped exactly what i wanted and then i closed the tab and it was just... gone. and so i'm sitting there like wait — the thing that knew the goal + the tradeoffs + why we picked postgres over the thing we almost picked, that all lived in a conversation i just deleted. that's when it clicked that the prompt was never the primitive. the conversation can't be the thing that remembers your company, right? the org has to. so orgx passes a compiled handoff instead of a transcript — goal, definition of done, decisions and why, budget, next action — and the work lives in a graph, not a thread that dies when you hit close. the prompt ends. what keeps moving after is the whole point and almost nobody's building for that part yet
concepts.cta: follow for more on building agents that outlive the chat
concepts.hypothesis: a thesis-declaration hook ('the prompt was the wrong primitive') + a quotable close beats a problem-first teardown for cold reach on a skeptical AI-infra audience

review.revised_hook_headline: the prompt was the wrong primitive.
issues: Slide 7 subtext 'follow if you're building agents that outlive the chat' + the separate cta field 'follow for more...' are the clean 'follow along' CTA endings the kill list explicitly bans. The caption nails the rough ending; the CTA slot undercuts it. Consider dropping the 'follow' framing and letting the quote 'the prompt ends. the company keeps moving.' stand alone.
Minor: 'durable.' as a one-word subtext on the graph slide is fine but borders on generic infra-adjective filler; 'receipts, not vibes' or a more specific tag would hit harder in-voice.
review.notes: Strong concept that satisfies the truth constraint fully — no fabricated metrics, traction, or benchmarks. All specificity is real-mechanism (compiled handoff fields, initiatives → milestones → tasks → receipts graph) which matches OrgX's actual architecture, plus a genuine building-in-public rep (the postgres decision) in the caption. That true specificity IS the credibility engine, exactly as required. Hook is genuinely contrarian for a skeptical AI-infra audience: attacking 'the prompt' as the wrong primitive challenges the thing everyone builds on. Caption is authentic Hope voice — opens on a specific tuesday realization, chains with 'and so'/'right?', drops 'postgres' casually, compresses with '+', narrates thinking live ('i'm sitting there like wait —'), and ends rough with no clean wrap. Slide text is tight, declarative, lowercase, sub-2s, one-idea-per-slide, zero kill-list words. The mechanism slide (five labeled fields) is the screenshot-worthy save asset. Only real weakness: the CTA slide + cta field lean on the 'follow if you're building...' close the voice guide bans — the caption avoids this trap but the CTA slot reintroduces it. Kept the original hook as the sharpest option; it's already the quotable thesis and any rewrite dilutes it. keep=true: accurate, overall 8 (>=7), hook 8 (>=6).

concepts.title: you approve every agent action — that's not oversight

slides.headline: you're the slowest agent in your fleet.
slides.subtext: and it's why the one risky action slips through.
slides.visual_note: a wall of glowing 'approve' buttons filling the frame, one red irreversible action buried in the middle, easy to miss.
slides.headline_original: reviewing every agent action isn't oversight. it's a bottleneck you built.

slides.headline: 50 agents. every one pings you. you became the queue.
slides.subtext: attention doesn't scale. activity does.
slides.visual_note: one human node in the center choking under ~50 inbound arrows all demanding a click.

slides.headline: so you rubber-stamp. and the action that mattered slips by.
slides.subtext: blanket approval is the same as no review.
slides.visual_note: approve buttons blurring into one motion-streak; a single red action passing straight through unnoticed.

slides.headline: human review should scale with consequence, not activity.
slides.subtext: reserve your attention for what can't be undone.
slides.visual_note: the pile of buttons collapses; a single clean gate remains at one doorway. everything quiets.

slides.headline: most agent work is reversible. let it run.
slides.subtext: drafts, plans, iterations — nobody needs to sign off on those.
slides.visual_note: a green 'auto' stream flowing freely down an open lane, no gates, no friction.

slides.headline: policy gates the boundary: publish · pay · message · merge.
slides.subtext: unknown high-risk tool? fails closed. every time.
slides.visual_note: instrument-panel: four gated actions in a row + a fail-closed padlock. clean, labeled, save-worthy.

slides.headline: everything else runs. you get the receipt, not the keystrokes.
slides.subtext: the artifact is the receipt.
slides.visual_note: a single clean proof/receipt card returning to the operator, not a scrolling log of every step.

slides.headline: escalate consequence, not activity.
slides.subtext: what would you actually want to approve? drop it below.
slides.visual_note: the line centered on dark; a faint comment-field shape at the bottom to bait replies.

concepts.caption: watched a founder friend demo his agent setup last month, he had like 30 agents running and he was so proud, and then i noticed he was clicking approve every four seconds. just approve approve approve. so i asked him when's the last time you actually said no, and he went quiet. right? because that's the tell — if you approve everything you're not reviewing anything, you're just a very slow queue with a pulse. and most of what agents do is reversible anyway. drafting, planning, iterating, who cares, let it run. the moment that actually needs you is the irreversible one: the publish, the payment, the merge, the message that can't be unsent. so gate THAT. tool policy sits at the boundary, unknown high-risk tool fails closed, everything else just moves. escalate consequence, not activity. your attention is the scarcest thing in the whole loop and you're burning it on keystrokes
concepts.cta: what would you actually want to approve? tell me below
concepts.hypothesis: a mistake-callout hook ('you approve every action — that's noise') + a save-bait tool-policy list drives more saves and comments than a pure thesis carousel

review.revised_hook_headline: you approve every agent action. that's not oversight — it's the bottleneck you built.
issues: Slide 6 subtext 'fails closed. every time.' — 'every time' is a mild slogan-y tic; borderline but acceptable.

Slides 3, 4, 6 stack aphorisms at the same declarative cadence ('blanket approval is the same as no review' / 'reserve your attention for what can't be undone') — not a kill-list violation, but the rhythm gets repetitive across the middle beats.
Caption ending 'you're burning it on keystrokes' lands a touch cleaner than the ideal rough/trailing Hope stop — minor.
review.notes:
Keep. Thesis 'escalate consequence, not activity' is genuinely contrarian and quotable, and every slide rides on REAL mechanisms: tool policy at the boundary, fail-closed on unknown high-risk tools, gating publish/pay/message/merge, artifact-as-receipt. Zero fabricated metrics, no user counts, no traction claims. The '50 agents'/'30 agents' figures are illustrative-hypothetical framing ('50 agents. every one pings you'), not claims about OrgX usage, and the visual note even hedges '~50' — so the truth constraint holds. Credibility comes from true specificity, exactly as required.

Hook (slide 1) 'reviewing every agent action isn't oversight. it's a bottleneck you built.' + 'and it's why the one risky action slips through' is a strong mistake-callout that implicates the reader — would stop a skeptical AI-founder mid-scroll. Slide 6 is the save-bait payload (four gated actions + fail-closed padlock, labeled instrument-panel) and earns the save; 'you get the receipt, not the keystrokes' is the second screenshot line.

Caption is real founder voice-memo: opens on a specific person/scene (friend demoing 30 agents, clicking approve every four seconds), chains with 'so/and/right?', narrates the realization ('so i asked him when's the last time you actually said no, and he went quiet'), drops mechanics casually without over-explaining. No kill-list words. Uses 'the loop' correctly. On-voice.

Slide text is tight, scannable, one idea per slide, lowercase-friendly, high conviction. No perfectly-parallel tricolons or em-dash 3-item lists in slides. The 'publish · pay · message · merge' middots read as a spec/instrument label, not a rhythmic tricolon — fine.

Why 8 not 9: the arc runs slightly long (three tension/proof beats doing similar work) and the middle slides share one aphoristic cadence. Coherent, differentiated, on-brand, solidly viral-capable for the contrarian-infra pillar. 'blanket approval is the same as no review' is a strong claim stated as fact, but it's thesis/opinion, not a fabricated metric — accuracy stands.

concepts.title: your agents don't have a memory problem, your org does

slides.headline: your agents remember fine. your org forgets.
slides.subtext: the fix isn't a bigger context window.
slides.visual_note: the word 'memory' crossed out, 'org' written underneath it, on near-black. stark, two words doing all the work.
slides.headline_original: your agents don't have a memory problem. your org does.

slides.headline: you hand off with a summary. the next agent inherits vibes.
slides.subtext: then re-derives everything you already decided.
slides.visual_note: a thin 3-line sticky-note summary being passed between two agent silhouettes across a gap.

slides.headline: it guesses the goal, guesses the bar, repeats the mistake.
slides.subtext: drift isn't a model flaw. it's a handoff flaw.
slides.visual_note: a task token quietly veering off a dotted 'intended path' line, no one watching.

slides.headline: a summary tells what happened. a handoff carries why it matters.
slides.subtext: why is the part that keeps work on the rails.
slides.visual_note: two cards side by side: a thin sparse 'summary' vs a dense, structured, fully-labeled 'handoff'.

slides.headline: what a real handoff actually carries:
slides.subtext: goal · definition of done · decisions+why · permissions · budget · confidence · next action
slides.visual_note: 7-field instrument panel, each field labeled and individually lit in a grid. THE save-bait slide — make it framework-clean.

slides.headline: below the quality bar? it loops back before it reaches you.
slides.subtext: the bar is owner-authored. versionable. yours.
slides.visual_note: a low-scoring artifact bouncing back into the loop instead of landing on the operator's desk.

slides.headline: the artifact returns with version, provenance, review state, receipt.
slides.subtext: you inspect proof, not process.
slides.visual_note: a proof-room card: version tag, source trace, eval score, receipt stamp — legible at a glance.

slides.headline: the next agent needs more than a summary.
slides.subtext: follow — i'm building the context layer under your AI clients.
slides.visual_note: the line centered; a faint row of client logos (claude, cursor, chatgpt) sitting on top of one shared context bar beneath them.

concepts.caption: someone told me their agents have a memory problem and i pushed back kind of hard, maybe too hard. because it's not the agents. the agents are fine. the problem is you handed off with a three-line summary and expected the next one to inherit an entire company's worth of context from it. a summary tells you what happened. it doesn't carry why it matters — the quality bar, the decision you already made and killed, the budget, the thing you're NOT allowed to do. so the next agent re-derives all of it from scratch and of course it drifts. that's not a memory problem, that's an org that never wrote down how it thinks. so we compile the handoff — goal + definition of done + decisions and why + permissions + budget + confidence + next action — and anything under the bar loops back before it ever hits your desk. the next agent needs more than a summary. it needs the org to actually remember
concepts.cta: follow — building the context layer under your AI clients
concepts.hypothesis: a reframe-diagnosis hook ('not a memory problem, an org problem') anchored by a 7-field handoff framework slide maximizes screenshot-saves vs a single-number payoff

review.revised_hook_headline: your agents don't have a memory problem. your org does.
issues: Slide 3 'it guesses the goal, guesses the bar, repeats the mistake' flirts with the banned parallel tricolon; it survives only because the third clause breaks the anaphora (repeats vs guesses). Fine as-is, but don't let it drift toward identical rhythm.

Caption ending 'it needs the org to actually remember' lands a touch cleaner than Hope's ideal rough trail-off since it echoes the thesis back — it's within bounds (no period, no 'follow along', no inspirational close) but is the least rough part of an otherwise perfect voice-memo.
Slide 8 CTA 'the next agent needs more than a summary' is slightly softer/more generic than the rest of the deck's conviction; the subtext carries it.
review.notes: Strong keep. The reframe-diagnosis hook is genuinely contrarian for an AI-founder audience ('memory problem' is the reflexive framing right now, and this flips it to an org/handoff problem), and it's anchored by a real, screenshot-worthy framework. Architecture verified against the live OrgX toolset: handoff_task, review_artifact/approve_agent_work (loop-back), orgx_submit_receipt (proof return), query_org_memory/recall_memory (context layer) — the 7-field handoff, owner-authored versionable bar, and version/provenance/review-state/receipt return are all real mechanisms, not invented specificity. Zero fabricated metrics. Voice is excellent in both registers: slides are billboard-tight ('inherits vibes', 'you inspect proof, not process', 'drift isn't a model flaw, it's a handoff flaw'), caption opens on a person + self-aware realization ('i pushed back kind of hard, maybe too hard'), chains with because/so, uses '+' compression on the handoff fields, drops specifics casually ('the decision you already made and killed'), and never hedges. No kill-list words. Slide 5 is the save engine and it earns it. Kept the original hook — it's the sharpest available and the crossed-out-'memory'/underlined-'org' visual depends on that exact swap; a tighter variant ('your agents don't forget, your org never wrote it down' — pulled from the caption's best line) is punchier but sacrifices the problem→problem symmetry that IS the concept spine, so not worth it. ONE watch-item, not an accuracy failure: slide 8's visual of claude/cursor/chatgpt logos on a shared context bar risks reading as 'these clients already run on OrgX.' It stays legal because the caption+CTA frame it as 'i'm building the context layer under your AI clients' (aspiration, not shipped integration) — keep the visual clearly conceptual/positioning, don't render it as a live integration badge, and it's clean.

concepts.title: I built OrgX on the wrong primitive

slides.headline: i built my agent system on the wrong primitive. for months.
slides.subtext: and i didn't notice until the handoff broke.
slides.visual_note: near-black #02040a, one huge line filling the frame. mono kicker 'confession' top-left with a thin amber underline. 'wrong primitive' is the only phrase weighted heavier.

slides.headline: every task started with a perfect prompt. and ended as a dead transcript.
slides.visual_note: two stacked fragments, second one dimmer/greyed like it's decaying. faint scroll of fake chat lines fading to nothing behind the text.

slides.headline: hand the work to the next agent and the 'why' was just gone.
slides.visual_note: danger-red accent only on the word 'gone'. a small broken-link / severed-thread glyph between two agent name chips (Eli → Sage).

slides.headline: the prompt was never the unit. the organization was.
slides.visual_note: the pivot slide. big iris statement, centered. everything above it in the deck should feel like it was building to this single line.

slides.headline: so i stopped passing transcripts. i started passing context packs.
slides.visual_note: split visual: left = crossed-out messy transcript block, right = a clean labeled 'context pack' card. teal accent on the pack side.

slides.headline: goal. definition of done. decisions + why. budget. next action.
slides.subtext: structured context handed over, not a chat log to re-read.
slides.visual_note: SAVE-BAIT slide. render as a tidy instrument-panel list, each field on its own row with a mono label. this is the one people screenshot.

slides.headline: the task outlives the chat now. that's the whole thing.
slides.visual_note: lime accent = it works. quiet, confident. small receipt/checkmark chip in the corner to signal 'shipped, real.'

slides.headline: what primitive are you still building on that you shouldn't be?
slides.subtext: building OrgX in public @ useorgx.com
slides.visual_note: soft close. question dominant, url small underneath. leave breathing room so it reads as an invitation to comment, not a pitch.

concepts.caption: the moment it clicked was watching a second agent pick up work a first one 'finished.' it had the files. it had the last message. and it still had no idea why any of it existed — what we'd already ruled out, what done even meant, who was allowed to approve the thing. and so i'm like, wait, i've been treating the prompt as the unit this whole time, but the prompt ends and the company doesn't. the goal + the decisions + the quality bar have to live somewhere that isn't a chat window that evaporates the second the context runs out. so orgx became the layer underneath claude / cursor / codex instead of another place to go type — a context pack instead of a transcript dump. i changed my mind about the most basic thing i was building on and honestly everything after it got simpler. still ripping out the old assumptions though
concepts.hypothesis: a confession/mistake hook plus a 'wrong primitive' reframe earns more founder trust + saves than a feature-list hook does for cold reach

review.revised_hook_headline: i built my agent system on the wrong primitive. shipped it that way for months.
issues: Minor: slide 8 CTA question ('what primitive are you still building on that you shouldn't be?') edges toward a tidy engagement-bait close, but it reads as a genuine invitation not a pitch, so it passes.
Nitpick: slide 6 subtext 'not a chat log to re-read' is slightly explanatory for billboard register — could be cut to let the field list stand alone, but it's fine.
review.notes:
Strong concept. Caption is textbook Hope voice-memo: opens on a concrete scene (second agent picking up 'finished' work), chains with 'and so i'm like, wait', drops claude/cursor/codex casually, compresses with 'the goal + the decisions + the quality bar', and ends rough ('still ripping out the old assumptions though'). No kill-list words. The line 'i've been treating the prompt as the unit this whole time, but the prompt ends and the company doesn't' is genuinely quotable and carries the whole reframe.

Accuracy is clean — this concept earns its credibility from architecture + felt problem, not invented traction. 'for months' refers to the founder's own build (truthful confession, not a metric). Real tools named, real mechanism (context pack: goal / definition of done / decisions+why / budget / next action). No fabricated users, revenue, funding, or benchmarks. Passes the truth constraint fully.

Slide arc is well-built: confession hook → tension (perfect prompt / dead transcript) → break (the 'why' was gone) → pivot ('the prompt was never the unit. the organization was.') → proof (context packs, the save-bait field list) → payoff ('the task outlives the chat') → soft question CTA. The 'wrong primitive' framing is the right kind of contrarian for a skeptical AI-founder — it's a technical-taste claim, not a growth brag, which is exactly what earns builder trust.

On the hook: keeping slide 1 as 'my agent system' rather than 'OrgX' is the correct cold-reach call — OrgX means nothing to a cold viewer, 'my agent system' is universally relatable. My revision only adds a touch of building-in-public sting ('shipped it that way') to raise the confession stakes without spoiling the slide-4 reframe; the original is already strong and either works. Keep it.

slides.headline: am i wrong that everyone's solving memory when the bug is continuity?
slides.subtext: building it in public @ useorgx.com

concepts.caption: someone in my dms was like 'i just need my agents to remember more' and i sat with that for a second, because i used to think the exact same thing. and so we all keep reaching for the same fix — bigger context window, better retrieval, tighter summaries — and the agent still shows up to the next task not knowing what we already decided or what 'done' even means here. right? and that's when it hit me that remembering the conversation and remembering the work are two completely different problems. memory is 'what did we say.' continuity is 'why does this exist + what's the bar + who approves.' the chat can't hold that, because the chat ends. so orgx keeps it in a work graph underneath the client — decisions, approvals, receipts, the quality bar — and the agents read from that instead of from a transcript. anyway i keep watching people optimize the memory and skip the continuity entirely

review.revised_hook_headline: your agents don't have a memory problem.
issues: Caption contains an em-dash parenthetical 3-item list ('the same fix — bigger context window, better retrieval, tighter summaries — and the agent still shows up') which is explicitly on the kill list. Fix: dissolve it into the run-on ('bigger context window, then better retrieval, then tighter summaries, and the agent STILL shows up') so it reads as talking, not a formatted aside.

Slide 1 uses the negation-hook template ('you don't have an X problem'), which is slightly formulaic — the specificity of 'memory' + the 'wrong bug' subtext rescue it, but it's the one spot that reads pattern-matched rather than found.
review.notes:
KEEP. This is the strongest kind of BIP concept: a real contrarian thesis (memory vs continuity) grounded in ACTUAL OrgX architecture — work graph, initiative→workstream→task→decision→receipt, approvals, the quality bar. Every one of those maps to real mechanisms, so the specificity is doing the credibility work, not invented traction. No fabricated metrics, no user counts, no fake benchmarks. The 'someone in my dms' opener is a soft anecdote-device, not a testimonial or a traction claim — low risk, standard BIP, leave it. If you want to be extra-clean you could make it 'someone asked me' but it's not required.

Hook: the open-loop design is smart — slide 1 negates the assumed problem, subtext ('patching the wrong bug') holds the loop, and 'continuity' isn't revealed until the turn. Don't let a redesign collapse hook+subtext onto slide 1; the delay is what makes slide 4 land. I kept the original headline — I couldn't beat it without either breaking the loop or getting wordier than a billboard allows.

Save-worthy line: slide 4's MEMORY vs CONTINUITY column + the caption's 'memory = what did we say / continuity = why does this exist + what's the bar + who approves' is the screenshot. Make sure the slide carries that same '+' breakdown, not just the two words.

Only real fix before ship: the em-dash 3-item list in the caption (kill-list violation). One-line edit.

Strong keep. No fabricated metrics, user counts, revenue, or benchmarks anywhere. The 300/400 message numbers are illustrative of the pain scenario, not claimed OrgX traction — legitimate.

VOICE: Slide text is billboard-tight and declarative. 'isn't a handoff. it's a hazard.' and 'inherits judgment, not a novel to re-read' are both quotable and in-register. Caption opens on a specific realization, chains with so/and/like, drops '300-message transcript' and 'why + the bar' casually, and ends rough on 'the compiled context is' — no clean wrap, no CTA close. My revised headline just tightens the slightly awkward 'i hand between agents' to 'between my agents' while keeping the 'zero transcripts' gold — marginal, original is already strong.

SAVE: Slide 4 (the full 7-field instrument-panel) is a genuine screenshot-worthy framework, and the CTA correctly points back to it. The framework carries the whole piece.

review.notes: Strong keep. Save-bait is real: the 7-field context-pack card ('goal · definition of done · decisions + why · permissions · budget · confidence · next action') is a genuine screenshot framework, and slide 5 correctly isolates 'decisions + why' as the load-bearing field, which sharpens rather than dilutes. Caption is the strongest asset — full Hope voice-memo, story-first, casual specificity, rough trail-off ending. Hypothesis (mistake-callout + numbered anatomy for saves+shares) is sound. Two fixes before shipping: (1) verify the 7-field spec is actually how OrgX compiles handoffs — if it's aspirational, keep it framed as the design thesis, not an existing feature; (2) the final CTA slide's 'follow for the next teardown' is the one clean-close/AI-growth-tell in an otherwise rough-edged piece — cut it or roughen it to match the caption's ending. concepts.caption: i counted the approval prompts my agents threw at me one afternoon and it was somewhere north of 40. forty times i clicked 'yes go ahead' on stuff like 'can i read this file' and 'can i write this draft.' and by like the fifth one i'm rubber-stamping without reading, which means the one approval that actually mattered — an agent about to send an email to a client — got the exact same reflexive yes as reading a file. that's the failure. human review should scale with consequence, not activity. so the escalation model in orgx just flips it: reversible stuff runs, and the gate only closes on the handful of things you can't take back. publishing, payments, outbound messages, merges. and anything high-risk it's never seen fails closed instead of guessing. it's not fewer guardrails, it's guardrails that are actually where the danger is...

review.notes:
Strong concept, ships. Truth check: the 'north of 40' count is a personal founder anecdote (felt problem), NOT fabricated OrgX traction — clean. No user counts, revenue, benchmarks, or testimonials invented. The only claim to watch is the specific mechanism list on slide 5 ('gates 4 things: publishing · payments · outbound messages · merges') and 'fails closed on unknown high-risk tools' — these are presented as real architecture/design decisions, which is the credibility engine when true. They must match OrgX's actual tool-policy/escalation model verbatim before publish; if the real system gates a different set, fix the list rather than the framing. Nothing here is a metric fabrication, so accurate stays true.

Voice: caption is genuinely in-register — opens on a specific realization, chains with 'and so', narrates thinking in real time ('by like the fifth one i'm rubber-stamping'), drops the client-email detail casually via a single em-dash insertion (not a banned 3-item em-dash list), and ends rough on '...'. No kill-list words. Slides are tight and declarative.

Why it works: 'escalate consequence, not activity' (slide 4) is the screenshot line and the spine of the whole deck — it's contrarian, memorable, and 100% on-thesis for governed autonomy. 'the bottleneck you automated away' (slide 2) is a second saveable line. Hook is a real, widely-shared pain (approval fatigue on agent fleets) framed as a design failure, not a UX gripe — that reframe is what stops a skeptical AI-founder.

Revised hook rationale: the original ('approving every agent action is the reason you can't run a fleet') is good but abstract. The revision pulls the caption's sharpest realization forward — the concrete count plus the curiosity gap ('which one? why?') — which is stickier and still fully true. Minor polish: consider swapping 'scale' → 'run' or 'compound' in the title to stay in Hope's word-set; and soften the CTA's URL drop so the piece doesn't tonally lurch from voice-memo into ad on the last slide.

concepts.caption: the moment that made me start OrgX: i had six agents running, the dashboard was all green, and i felt worse than when i was doing everything by hand. activity went straight up and my confidence went straight down, and those two aren't supposed to move opposite directions but they do the second you're orchestrating instead of typing. and i figured out why — every unit of work was asking for the same slice of my attention. a typo fix and a payment endpoint both showing up as 'review me', same size, same weight. so you either rubber-stamp the batch and something irreversible slips through, or you re-read all of it and you're the bottleneck again, congrats you built an expensive way to do your own job. the fix was never more review. it's escalating consequence, not activity — reserve the human boundary for the stuff that can't be undone, publish, payments, merges, and let everything under the bar loop back before it hits your desk. that's the whole difference between autonomy that quietly stresses you out and autonomy that actually

concepts.caption: i counted it once — in a single afternoon i re-explained the same feature goal five separate times. once handing from Claude to Codex, once when i moved repos, once switching to Cursor for the frontend, once to a teammate, and once to myself on friday when i'd already forgotten why i started. and none of those were 'losing' context in some vague way, they were five specific doors and the context died at every single one. that's when it clicked that context isn't a transcript you scroll back through hoping the answer's buried in there, it's a graph the org is supposed to keep — the decisions, the approvals, the artifacts, the receipts, living underneath the client instead of trapped inside one conversation. because the task outlives the chat, always, the chat is the most disposable part of the whole thing and somehow we made it the primitive. so i'm building the layer that sits beneath Claude and Cursor and Codex and just remembers — the goal, the bar, the why — so every door is a pass-through instead of a reset. anyway. five times in one afternoon, that number still

issues: Slide 6 'initiatives · decisions · approvals · artifacts · receipts — durable, not disposable' brushes against the banned list-with-dash pattern; it survives because it's a 5-item product-primitive enumeration (not a 3-item em-dash parenthetical) and the dash carries a real declarative payoff, but it's the one slide that reads as a spec sheet instead of a billboard.
CTA slide's 'which door kills yours?' is lightly engagement-baity/clean; it's saved by being a genuine comment prompt tied to the door motif, but it's the closest the deck gets to a tidy CTA.

Caption has two stacked parallel lists ('the decisions, the approvals, the artifacts, the receipts' then 'the goal, the bar, the why') — embedded in flowing prose so it reads as real speech, not rhetorical tricolon, but it's the one spot rhythm gets slightly showy.
review.notes:
Truth: clean. The '5 doors' are real workflow boundaries (Claude→Codex→Cursor, repo, client, today-you→next-week-you) and the proof slide names real OrgX primitives (initiatives/decisions/approvals/artifacts/receipts as a durable graph beneath the client). Zero fabricated traction — the 'i counted it once, five times in one afternoon' is a personal building-in-public rep, not an invented metric. Credibility engine is doing its job.

Voice: caption is the strongest asset — story-first open ('i counted it once'), chains with and/so/because, drops tools casually, narrates the click in real time ('that's when it clicked that context isn't a transcript...'), and ends genuinely ROUGH ('anyway. five times in one afternoon, that number still'). No kill-list words. Slides are tight and lowercase-friendly. 'you are the fifth agent. and you forgot the why too.' is the standout gut-punch. 'context isn't a chat you scroll back through. it's a graph the org keeps' and 'the task outlives the chat' are the quotable spines.

Hook: strong reframe (leaking→dying at N specific doors) + personal 'this week' tag stops a skeptical scroll. The concept's own hypothesis concedes a slightly slower first-slide read vs a single emotional scene — fair, but the payoff of a numbered save-bait list is worth it. My revised headline subverts the exact phrase founders say ('I lost context') and folds the temporal punch into the headline itself, so the hook lands before the subtext loads — marginal but real improvement; original is already keep-worthy.

Verdict: keep. High brand fit (this IS the OrgX thesis), high save potential (numbered framework + two quotable lines + a real product-state screenshot on slide 6), and it closes the loop visually (thread thinning at each door → passing through at full strength). One watch item: make slide 6 actually feel like real product state in render, or it reads as a feature list and undercuts the 'durable, not disposable' claim.

The credibility comes from a true mental model (the 7-step loop), the real thesis ('the conversation was never supposed to remember, the org is'), and a real felt problem (agent handoff amnesia) — exactly the sanctioned credibility engine, not invented traction. Slide 5 is a genuine screenshot artifact and the numbered-loop format is purpose-built save-bait, matching the stated hypothesis. concepts.caption: someone asked me last week how I actually trust the work my agents ship and I gave this long answer and then realized the honest version is way simpler: I don't trust 'done.' 'done' is a vibe. an agent typing a checkmark at the end of a transcript tells me nothing about whether the thing is right. and the two options everyone defaults to are both bad, either I re-check all of it myself which means I didn't really delegate, or I trust it blind which is how you wake up to something ugly in prod. so the reframe that actually fixed it: the artifact is the receipt. work comes back with its version + provenance + how it was evaluated + what review state it's in, proof I can read in ten seconds without re-running anything. and the stuff under the quality bar loops back before it ever reaches my attention, and it shows up right inside Claude or Cursor so I'm not leaving to go verify. still think most agent tools are shipping vibes not receipts but

issues: CTA subtext 'the proof layer is OrgX. follow for how it's built.' is a soft follow-CTA — brushes against the no-'follow along'/no-clean-CTA rule. Acceptable on a CTA slide but it's the least in-voice line in the set.

Proof slide 'version. provenance. evaluation. review state. the receipt.' — the trailing 'the receipt.' is redundant with the kicker 'WHAT A RECEIPT CARRIES' and softens an otherwise perfect screenshot line. Cut it.
review.notes: Strong concept, dead-center brand fit. No fabricated metrics/traction/testimonials — every claim is architecture or felt-problem: receipts, quality bar, review state, loop-back, embedded MCP widgets in Claude/Cursor/Codex all map to real OrgX mechanisms (orgx_submit_receipt, review_artifact, the 0.85 quality gate, rework loop). 'done is a vibe' is a memeable, of-the-moment reframe (rides 'vibe coding' discourse) that will stop a skeptical AI-founder. 'the artifact is the receipt' + the four-field list is genuinely screenshot-able framework content. Caption is authentic Hope: opens on a person/realization, chains with and/so/but, uses '+' compression ('version + provenance + how it was evaluated + what review state it's in'), drops Claude/Cursor casually, ends rough mid-sentence ('...shipping vibes not receipts but'). No kill-list words. Two watch-items: (1) verify 'proof rooms' is a real OrgX product term and not an invented feature name — it describes real embedded MCP widgets so it's not a fabrication, but if the name isn't shipped, swap to plain 'the proof shows up inside the client' to stay honest. (2) Revised hook compresses three sentences into one concrete line ('typed' beats 'said' — matches the checkmark-in-a-transcript image) and lands 'vibe, not proof' harder; original is fine if you want the setup beat. keep: true (accurate, overall 8, hook 8).

hook_rules: ≤8 words, largest text on the slide, lowercase, Inter — the hook must survive as a standalone screenshot.
State a falsifiable tension, not a claim: a named failure, a flat contrarian thesis, or a real artifact with something conspicuously missing. Never '10x', never superlatives, never emoji.
The hook must open a loop the viewer cannot resolve without swiping — and the payoff must honestly land on slide n-1, never rug-pull to a docs link.
If the concept has a real number (3 tiers, 5 doors, 7 fields), put the number in the hook — it sets the completion contract. Never invent a number.
Never open with the product name, logo, or 'OrgX' — brand presence on slide 1 is the small wordmark on the continuity line, nothing more.
Use second person and the reader's exact situation ('you're pasting the whole chat...') or first-person building-in-public ('7 fields i hand between agents') — never third-person marketing voice.
Every word must be defensible in an adversarial dev comment section; if a claim needs a caveat, it's not the hook.
Prefer the two-beat structure: setup, hard turn ('your agents remember. your org forgets.') — the turn is what stops the thumb.

viral_principles.principle: Slide 1 is a distribution decision — the algorithm gates cold reach on swipe-through from the first slide, and skeptical devs pattern-match hype and scroll.
viral_principles.apply: Every carousel opens with a flat, falsifiable 5-8 word thesis or named failure in the instrument aesthetic. Never the product name, never a claim with a number we can't defend. A/B the three hook archetypes weekly in the learner loop: failure-story, thesis, artifact-render.

viral_principles.principle: Slide 2 is where carousels die — it must escalate, never restate or warm up.
viral_principles.apply: Lock the 8-slide house format: (1) hook, (2) escalation — the concrete cost or failure, (3) reframe ('the prompt ends. the company keeps moving.'), (4-6) one mechanism per slide rendered as real UI, (7) numbered framework recap, (8) payoff + in-platform CTA. Kill any format where slide 1→2 drop-off exceeds ~40%.

viral_principles.principle: Open a specific loop on slide 1, pay it off on slide n-1 — the last slide is CTA/recap, and the loop must be honest or the audience blocks you.
viral_principles.apply: Use OrgX's real taxonomies as loops: 'reversible, ambiguous, irreversible — only one should reach you' resolves on slide 7. Design one deliberately dense diagram per carousel (the context-pack schema or escalation ladder) to farm reverse-swipes, which are a tracked ranking signal.

viral_principles.principle: Saves come from reference density; shares come from identity — engineer them on DIFFERENT slides.
viral_principles.apply: Every carousel carries exactly one save slide (the context pack as a copyable config-file schema in JetBrains Mono, or the run/ask/gate decision tree) and one share slide (a single large-type quotable line a founder DMs to a cofounder). Never combine them.

viral_principles.principle: Numbered frameworks are the save-rate engine and set a completion contract — but only real taxonomies survive dev comments.
viral_principles.apply: Put the number in the slide-1 hook and show n/8 progress in mono chrome. Rotate OrgX's genuine numbers: 3 consequence tiers, 7 context-pack fields, the proof-return components, 7 named agents. Never pad a listicle.

viral_principles.principle: External-link CTAs suppress reach; in-platform CTAs (save/send/follow) multiply it.
viral_principles.apply: Final slide: framework echo + 'save this before your next agent handoff.' + useorgx.com set small in mono as panel chrome (provenance, not a pitch). Real link lives in bio and pinned comment. Never write 'link in bio' on a slide. Design slide 8→1 as a loop seam for TikTok's photo-mode looping.

viral_principles.principle: For skeptical devs the anti-hype register IS the hook — product mechanisms rendered as real UI beat marketing graphics.
viral_principles.apply: Every visual depicts a mechanism, never a metaphor and never a metric. One defensible-but-debatable claim per carousel (e.g. 'autonomy without a quality bar is delegation to a stranger') with technically deeper replies prepared — adversarial comment velocity is distribution.

viral_principles.principle: Named recurring characters drive follows, and follows compound the reach of every subsequent post in a daily cadence.
viral_principles.apply: Run Pace, Eli, Mark, Sage, Orion, Dana, Xandy as a serialized cast of runtime chips (never mascots). Episode mark 'FLEET LOG ###' in mono chrome; incident-narrative carousels told through product frames; pinned intro-the-fleet carousel as profile anchor.

viral_principles.principle: TikTok photo mode is its own animal: audio is quasi-mandatory (~70% reach penalty without), slide text is OCR'd, captions are search queries.
viral_principles.apply: Design 9:16-native (1080x1920) with text out of the right 120px rail and bottom 320px. Rotate 2-3 ambient/lo-fi sounds that fit the matte-HUD vibe. Write captions as searchable dev queries: 'agent handoff context pack', 'run multiple AI agents without losing context'. Export a recomposed 4:5 crop for IG.

tiktok.safe_zones: keep key text out of the bottom 320px and right 120px (TikTok UI overlay)
tiktok.audio: attach an ambient/lo-fi sound in the TikTok app before publishing — photo-mode posts without audio take a large reach penalty
tiktok.caption: write captions as searchable dev queries (e.g. "agent handoff context pack"); real link in bio + pinned comment, never "link in bio" on a slide
tiktok.cta: final slide CTA is save/send/follow language; useorgx.com appears as small panel chrome, not a pitch

visual_vocabulary.name: Continuity Line
visual_vocabulary.concept: the thesis itself — the prompt ends, the company keeps moving
visual_vocabulary.when: every slide, no exceptions — it is the signature and the narrative spine

visual_vocabulary.name: Agent Chip
visual_vocabulary.concept: named agents as a fleet (Pace, Eli, Mark, Sage, Orion, Dana, Xandy)
visual_vocabulary.when: any handoff, escalation, or FLEET LOG episode slide

visual_vocabulary.name: Context Pack Card
visual_vocabulary.concept: compiled handoff (goal, definition of done, decisions+why, permissions, budget, confidence, next action)
visual_vocabulary.when: any handoff/context topic; full-size on the dense reference slide of context-pack carousels

visual_vocabulary.name: Transcript Scatter
visual_vocabulary.concept: the failure state — pasting the whole chat as a handoff
visual_vocabulary.when: max one state-pair per carousel; before/after prompt-vs-org, transcript-vs-pack

visual_vocabulary.name: Handoff Break
visual_vocabulary.concept: the prompt ends — context dies between agents
visual_vocabulary.when: tension/escalation slides (slide 2) and 'felt' concepts like felt-handoff-why

visual_vocabulary.name: Escalation Ladder
visual_vocabulary.concept: consequence-based escalation: reversible → run, ambiguous → ask, irreversible → gate
visual_vocabulary.when: escalation carousels; also the dense save slide when rendered with tier labels

visual_vocabulary.name: Gate Notch
visual_vocabulary.concept: tool policy gating publishing, payments, merges

visual_vocabulary.name: Quality Bar
visual_vocabulary.concept: owner-authored quality bar; sub-threshold work loops back
visual_vocabulary.when: quality-bar and 'done is a vibe' concepts

visual_vocabulary.name: Receipt Stub
visual_vocabulary.concept: proof return: artifact + version + provenance + evaluation + receipt
visual_vocabulary.when: any proof/receipt concept; the line's ending on proof-themed carousels

visual_vocabulary.name: Work Graph Fragment
visual_vocabulary.concept: the work graph: initiatives, tasks, agents, decisions, artifacts, receipts

visual_vocabulary.name: Five Doors
visual_vocabulary.when: the five-doors carousel only — it is that concept's bespoke device

visual_vocabulary.name: Loop Circuit
visual_vocabulary.concept: the 7-step loop every piece of real work runs
visual_vocabulary.when: the-loop-agents-break and recap slides for loop-themed carousels

visual_vocabulary.name: Memory Strata
visual_vocabulary.concept: shared operating context living beneath the clients, not in the chat

visual_vocabulary.name: HUD Frame + Index
visual_vocabulary.when: every slide; slides 2+ carry the n/8 index

visual_vocabulary.name: Verified Stamp (end card)
visual_vocabulary.concept: the quality-bar verdict / proof return, dramatized
visual_vocabulary.when: every carousel's final slide; GATED red stamp variant for irreversible-action episodes
